skill.name: github-multi-repo
skill.description: Coordinate GitHub work across multiple repositories with swarm-style planning, dependency synchronization, and architecture standardization workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and coordinate multi-repository GitHub updates, dependency alignment, templates, and release workflows.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, and environment patterns. Review found these are documentation examples for GitHub and repository automation, not hidden executable code, but they can trigger broad writes, pushes, pull requests, and package changes if followed by an agent. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Broad Cross-Repository Shell Automation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill documents Bash workflows that enumerate repositories, clone them into temporary paths, run package commands, commit changes, push branches, and create pull requests. This is expected for the skill, but it can modify many repositories if executed with a privileged GitHub token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are shown directly in the skill and perform repository writes and pull request creation. They appear legitimate for multi-repository automation, so the risk is operational misuse rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: GitHub API Write Operations
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples use GitHub API and GitHub MCP write operations to create refs, update package files, create template repositories, and push files. These actions need human review, branch restrictions, and least-privilege credentials before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented calls clearly write to GitHub resources. The context is an automation skill, which makes the behavior plausible but still sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Temporary Filesystem Use With Repository Names
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Several examples write repository lists and cloned repositories under /tmp using shell variables. This is common for automation, but unvalidated repository names and shared temporary paths can cause collisions or unintended file access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file and directory writes are explicit, but they are examples rather than bundled scripts. The main concern is unsafe execution without path validation.

medium_findings.title: Webhook Secret And External Coordination Endpoint
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill includes a webhook configuration that reads process.env.WEBHOOK_SECRET and sends events to an external coordinator URL. This is a plausible integration pattern, but users should ensure secrets are not logged or forwarded to untrusted endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment secret access and external URL are real, but the code shown does not prove credential exfiltration. Human review should verify the actual coordinator endpoint before use.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak Cryptography Alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The static weak-cryptography alerts correspond to metadata, branch SHA lookup, base64 content encoding for the GitHub API, path names, and related skill names. No weak hashing or encryption implementation was found in the skill text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines use terms such as sha, base64, and architect in non-cryptographic contexts. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1 password storage, or custom encryption.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs point to public documentation and issue pages for claude-flow. They are not used in an executable network request inside the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in the Support and Resources section. No evidence found that they receive credentials or repository data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Examples With Repository Write Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many Bash examples that can execute package installs, tests, commits, pushes, and pull request creation. These patterns are dangerous when run automatically with broad GitHub permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and can change remote repositories. The skill context makes them expected, so they require warnings rather than blocking.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Adjacent Webhook Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references an external coordinator URL together with an environment-sourced webhook secret. This is sensitive because incorrect endpoint selection could expose coordination data or secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret access is visible, but no code in the skill proves secret leakage. The risk depends on the endpoint used by the operator.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to keep dependencies, templates, tests, and documentation aligned across many repositories. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for planning and coordinating GitHub changes.
actual_capabilities: Plans coordinated changes across multiple GitHub repositories.
Documents workflows for dependency updates and version alignment.
Provides patterns for repository template creation and structure standardization.
Coordinates integration testing and pull request creation across related projects.
Uses swarm-style agent roles for architecture, analysis, coding, testing, and documentation.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI, repository access, and appropriate tokens for live automation.
Examples can modify repositories and should not run without human review.
Does not include a standalone executable implementation in this package.
Assumes users can adapt organization names, branch rules, and repository layouts.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Microservice Updates
use_cases.description: Plan dependency, contract, and test updates across several service repositories.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Templates
use_cases.description: Create consistent project files, workflows, and documentation across new or existing repositories.

use_cases.title: Prepare Organization-Wide Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Draft a controlled rollout plan for package upgrades, security patches, or documentation synchronization.

prompt_templates.title: Map Related Repositories
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to identify related repositories for this project. Summarize their roles, dependencies, and suggested coordination order.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Dependency Alignment
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to plan a dependency alignment across these repositories. Include test gates, branch names, and pull request sequencing.

prompt_templates.title: Standardize Repository Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to compare repository structures and propose a standard template. Flag files that need manual review before changes.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Security Patch Rollout
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to design a security patch rollout across all affected repositories. Require confirmation before any write operation.

output_examples.input: Plan a TypeScript version update across frontend, backend, and shared packages.
output: Repository roles and dependency order are listed.
A branch and pull request plan is proposed for each repository.
Test gates and rollback checks are included before merge.

output_examples.input: Standardize CI and documentation across five service repositories.
output: The skill groups repositories by role and current structure.
It recommends shared workflow and documentation templates.
It identifies files that require owner approval before updates.

output_examples.input: Prepare a cross-repository security patch workflow.
output: Affected repositories are prioritized by risk and dependency impact.
The rollout plan separates scanning, patching, testing, and review.
Human approval is required before pushing branches or creating pull requests.

best_practices: Run discovery and planning steps before allowing any write operation.
Use least-privilege GitHub tokens and protected branches for all automation.
Review generated diffs, tests, and pull request bodies before publishing changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run organization-wide commands with administrator tokens by default.
Do not clone or write repositories into shared temporary paths without isolation.
Do not synchronize documentation or templates without checking project-specific differences.

faq.question: Does this skill make repository changes by itself?
faq.answer: The package is documentation-driven, but its examples describe commands that can make live GitHub changes if executed.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: Live workflows need GitHub CLI access and tokens scoped to the repositories being updated.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with review gates, protected branches, and least-privilege credentials. Do not allow automatic writes without approval.

faq.question: Can it help with monorepo migration?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes planning patterns for analyzing repositories, preserving history, and preparing migration pull requests.

faq.question: Does it include prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No prompt injection text was found during this audit.
